title: Chris Andrew / Kirkland Gee - Scrunch <> GrowthX
meeting_title: Chris Andrew / Kirkland Gee - Scrunch <> GrowthX

transcript.text: Great role to have at GrowthX.
Exactly.
I assumed you're critical.
Very.
Hi.
Bridget.
Hi.
I'm Bridget.
I run the ops team at GrowthX.
And then I used to work in partnerships, actually.
So I'd mentioned that to Jason.
And he was like, oh, we should join these kind of calls.
here I am.
I was just watching you on a podcast like 30 seconds ago.
So it's kind of funny to see your face right now.
The whole new speaking publicly is the thing for me.
So thanks for listening.
It's like, it's a necessity.
It was like a travel, experience this travel, that one.
So I was kind of confused by the title.
Is this some kind of travel podcast?
Yeah.
They were a random reach out, but they've actually been a great partner.
Nice.
Taking the opportunities.
Got to get your reps in.
Yep.
Exactly.
Cool.
Awesome.
Yeah.
This will be everybody from our side for today, Chris.
So I appreciate you just taking a call.
To kind of explore what.
We could potentially do here.
Yeah.
I don't know if maybe you want us to start kind of explaining what we're trying to do or if you kind of want to start on your side.
don't know what's easiest.
Yeah.
Why don't you just give me like a couple minutes on what you're trying to do?
You mentioned you're hearing kind of needs and asks from across the customer base.
I've looked a little bit at the company.
know the category, but maybe tell me a little bit about the business and kind of scale and what we're looking to solve for.
And then I can talk through what we're doing and also kind of what we're doing in this growing niche of kind of being a data provider for partners and growth players and programmatic SEO shops.
So, yeah, why we start there?
Yeah.
You want me to take it away correctly?
Yeah, Jason, I think you probably can answer that best.
Okay, cool.
So, I mean, it looks like you looked at GrowthX ready and basically, you know, you can view us like a growth agency on steroids.
You know, we combine kind of under the hood, a lot of AI and a lot of people.
And the point is like customers pay for an outcome.
We just figure it out.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Um, and and
Probably the biggest, you know, by far the biggest thing we do for customers today is around SEO.
And all of them are asking us, you know, what are you guys doing around AI SEO?
I would say that the stage of our customers are all over the place.
Some of them use you guys, some of them use Profound, some of them like build stuff themselves.

And then on our end, I think it's just really important to offer the ability to track kind of clear direction on what we're doing to actually like get ahead of this, you know, to help them be cited more.
And then increasingly so, at least in our view, like distribution to get your content so you're not just talking about yourself is like critical as well.
And, you know, like all these things, like we want to package together so we can help our customers, basically.
What you mean by distribution?
Kind of like, so we create a lot of content and then, you know, distribution for us means...

Like, you know, repurposing for socials, like trying to get them mentioned in other places, getting, you know, backlinks, but not maybe in the traditional sense, just paying for links, but having kind of very organic outreach to get mentions.
Because it seems like that, you know, at least for citations happens a lot.
feel like we've been seeing a little bit, like if you just kind of talk about yourself, like I've seen a lot of instances where like that just doesn't really get included, even if you're ranked.
Quite high.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So I guess that's kind of our need.
Yeah.
mean, I played around a bunch with Profound.
I think I'm just super curious, like your view and then what you guys are doing and then how we can work together.
Basically.
Yeah.
You know.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So if you, are you guys customers of Profound today?
You mentioned you've played around with it.
No, we aren't.
But like our customers are.
Our customers are.
They'll just let us in and we can help them manage it in a lot of cases.
So.
Yeah, nice.

And I guess just to think about, I mean, if you could wave a magic wand and you'd have like a solution to this for your client base, I could speculate on what I think it looks like based on what I'm seeing in the market, but maybe articulate what your ideal outcome would I might be able to speak to that a little bit because eventually, probably within the next three months-ish, we'll probably have our own sort of proprietary performance reporting dashboards built into some other software that we're building.
Trying to make that all kind of one place.
Three months may be aggressive.
It may be longer than that before we have our own custom solution.

But the idea would be the same place we're looking at their GSC data, the same place we're looking at everything else to have a kind of all-in-one dashboard that has the context of all the work we're doing.
And allows them to understand the impact of that.
Yep.
Not just on performance, also on kind of the AI side of things, from citations all the way through attribution.
Right.
Yes, we were mentioned this much in AI, and then we got this many referral visits.
And then that many blah, blah, blah, blah.
That's like the eventual goal right now.
That probably looks like Looker Studio in the meantime.
And I assume on the attribution side, you're already tracking that today somehow through Google Analytics, Looker, Adobe.
Yeah, depending on the client, but typically just referral visits in GA is going to be the most common.
Gotcha.
Cool.
Yeah, great, great background.
So quick background on me and the business.
I'm Chris Andrew, co-founder and CEO for Scrunch.
We started the company like 18 months ago, kind of before any of these platforms had web search.
I was like, this is where the world's going.
Humans want answers, not links, blah, blah, blah.
Started building, web search comes.
It's been manic the last kind of four or five months.
Obviously, super busy category right now, like profound.
I think they came out maybe October of last year.
We announced maybe a month and a half after.
And then I think there's been 30 players that have jumped in.
So I've never really participated in a market like this.
My overall view is that monitoring is a commodity.
I'll be very clear about that.
think every business needs to understand their presence, their position, their sentiment, their citations, track that in a very granular way over time.
And we are using monitoring as a wedge in and we're building out a number of other capabilities that we think are going to be more differentiated and valuable.
think monitoring is going to be a super interesting, valuable standalone business, but there's more.
With that in mind, you know, we've been growing in the market.
So we've got your traditional like Fortune 500 enterprise SaaS deals that are like 50K to 100K a year.
And then we've got more mid-market deals that are month to month.
So just trying to be really accessible, get you in with like a month to month package.
Could be $300 a month, could be $1,000 a month based on volume of what you need.
I'll show you the platform here in just a second.
We've carved out...
Second kind of line of business, if you will, in this fledgling world of basically being the data provider for folks like yourselves, where in some cases we end up overlapping with clients.

I've got some partners where the partner, another growth agent, is paying for a feed of data for their client, and their client is also paying for a login of our product, so they have direct access and a little bit more hands-on control.
I think that's going to happen in this space, and honestly, I'm fine with it.
And then there are some companies that fully outsource to folks like yourselves, and they'll never buy technology.
And obviously, that's a perfect partnership situation for us, because there's zero competitive overlap, and you guys should be enabled to do everything on their behalf.
I think what I'm seeing in the market is, you know, we're working with probably 60-plus companies now, like having started selling in late January, basically.
I think we had one customer in December, and now it's, like, gotten pretty crazy.
And then...
then...
It's admin market and enterprise.
And then I think of this kind of data effort as a little bit separate from what we're doing.
For our data partners, we're kind of looking at an API.
A of them are using Looker, like a Looker Studio instance to take that data in, but you can take it in whatever format you'd like.
There's kind of four key data points that most organizations are taking, which are presence, position, and web position.
We kind of average it.
We're like, are they on the top of the results, the middle of the results, the bottom of the results, if they're present at all, sentiment towards the organization, and then comprehensive citations.
So for the themes you're monitoring, for your brand, like branded and non-branded prompts, what are the sites that are being cited in the answers?
And this is covering GPT, perplexity, AI overviews, Meta, Claude, with WebSearch right now, we haven't turned on WebSearch, Claude, and then Gemini is going live in May.
So comprehensive coverage.
Thank you.
Some data partners are like, look, GPT represents 90% of this.
Let's just get started with that.
And we're doing this with a number of companies.
Daydream has announced it publicly, so I'll just mention that.
They're kind of like, look, we have AI search covered for our companies.
We're just baking it into every contract.
And as you described, it's like, let's just get this embedded into core dashboards so that we have an answer and a story.
And then on the other end, you're looking at the insights and action and gaps.
You're like, look, we need to create content or from a distribution perspective, get our content in other places because AI is no longer just looking at my website.
We're looking at 10 sources to decide whether to mention me or not.
So maybe that gives you a little sense of what we're doing.
I'm happy to give a quick walkthrough of the interface product if that's helpful.
I mean, maybe just one question.
You said the monitoring is kind of a wedge.
Like for you guys, what does that mean?
Like where are you guys building more into?
Yeah, you'll see a little bit of it in the product.
But I think, you know, the goal for us isn't to be like the SEMrush of the future.
I think companies are going to need a completely different marketing stack to successfully operate in a world where humans are not visiting websites.
And so I think what that gets into is a little bit more infrastructure to manage crawlers and agents.
To be like, okay, these GPT users are arriving at my website.
What are they looking for?
Give it to them.
Like what they should not have access to this content.
we're experimenting in that category.
We're doing a bit of experimentation in, you know, probably similar areas to you.
think there's going to be a lot of overlap, like cooperation, cooperation, if you will, where it's like, look, we've written all of this content for Google or humans.
And we should be writing content for AI crawlers and LLMs.
Like what's the way that an LLM retrieves content?
What is it prioritizing?
What is it looking at, looking at huge swaths of data to make sense of the content that needs to be created if that's the primary consumer.
So you can imagine.

And us also participating in that down the road, which I think is good to be clear about because we're never going to be a growth end-to-end platform, but we may have organizations think these are pages and assets that need to exist.
I think content creation is also kind of a commodity in the AI world, but what goes into it based on insights is differentiated.
And that's probably why you guys win.
You know what to be creating at scale.
And we, in an AI segment, have some of those insights as well, and we'll probably look at creating in that bucket.
So all of that requires some sense of monitoring to understand how you're performing and what you can do about it.
But I think there's more interesting things to be building on top of it.
Okay, that makes sense.
And then, I don't want to ask all the questions, but one more.
I guess the one change in thinking for this citation mentioned versus SEMrush's traditional keywords, with keywords, you're kind of aware of the universe a little bit.
But with this one, there's kind of an art and a judgment to just pick.
Like, don't know how many prompts a typical customer tracks, but like 500 or 1000, like what those are.
Like, like, where do you think that goes?
Like, are you always kind of just like, almost half guessing at the intents?
I mean, I guess you get some clues from search, right?
What the intents are, but yeah, it's a great question.
think like, um, got a million thoughts on it.
could probably spend an hour just on this.
I we built a prompt generation platform that looks at a ton of inputs.
So right, we're looking at your expected third-party data input.
So Google AdWord keyword data, that's publicly available.
Now, the traditional Google search is three to four words.
The traditional prompt is like 40 to 70 words, right?
It's kind of crazy how different that is.
I've seen some very elementary attempts by consultants and other vendors in the space to be like, here's your volume of AI search activity.
And it's like, take Google search volume and divide it by 95%.
It's like, I actually don't think that's representative or indicative of what's happening on GPT.
The other thing.
I've seen people do is you buy clickstream data, panel data from the likes of SimilarWeb or Datos, and you try to multiply it out, right?
You're like, hey, we have some clickstream data of like what people are doing on GPT or complexity, people who are paid to share their browser history or incentivized to download an app.
And then you can view all of their activity on the web.
We've purchased those data sets.

My view of them is that they're really, really misleading because they're heavily VPNed users coming from India, China, people who are paid to share browser history or downloaded an app without knowing they're sharing everything on their device.
They're rarely representative of a buyer, right?
So like I'm seeing two worlds.
can either triangulate based on traditional search.
You can buy third-party clickstream data and try to multiply it out.
And we've purchased the data sets and looked at both.
And I would not feel confident about representing a volume to any buyer that is dictating significant strategy.
What we are doing...

Is taking that input, plus a ton of other inputs, our own customer interview data, like what are prompt structures looking like, as much third-party data as we can, and generating prompts at scale for our customers based on themes.
Personas are a huge part of our platform.
Like who does, I mean, name a customer for me.
Like give me an example of somebody you guys work with.
You know, Webflow.
Webflow.
Love the Webflow team.
Bryant, one of the founders, is actually an investor in Scrunch, a good old friend of mine.
I know Vlad very well.
started our career at Intuit together.
So them, right, they're like, who do they sell to?
Well, they're trying to move from selling to the scrappy contractor design agency to a Fortune 500 IT team.
Those are two very different buyers who are asking very different questions and getting very different answers.
And so our platform at the core has this persona concept where you're actually mapping prompts to personas and seeing the variation and the answers that are coming back.
And so I'll show you the product in a sec, but you're going to filter back.
Like personas, you're going to filter by stages of the customer journey, like advice and awareness.
People are not asking about growthx, but they're struggling with leads, right?
Do you guys show up when people are struggling with leads?
Obviously, you're going to show up when it's a comparison prompt of like growthx versus this.
One of the things I think that's weird about this space is I definitely see vendors gaming outcomes because depending on the prompt you ask, you can make a brand show up or not.
And so it's like, is your goal is actually conversion, like attribution, leads, outcomes.
If all you want to do is make someone feel good about their presence in AI search, just ask different prompts and you can game it.
And so I think we've actually seen some companies switch to our platform in the last month from other leading players.
And they're just like, whoa, like I thought we were here in AI search.
But when I went back and actually looked, I couldn't create my own prompts.
And so I'm looking at like category prompts, industry prompts, branded prompts, and it was way out of whack in terms.
Like how we're actually showing up.
So I think it's just super early in this space and you need a lot of granularity in that creation process.
Cool.
That makes sense.
Yeah.
Any other questions?
No.
I mean, I guess if we could look at a quick preview.
I'm curious, have you guys explored building this yourselves?
Have you talked to anybody else that can help you provide data?
Not really.
mean, I don't think we're really planning to build it ourselves.
I guess.
Yeah, we briefly talked about it.
I mean, to your point, the monitoring piece of it would not be like an incredibly difficult thing.
There's the simple of like, hey, to track our presence day over day for X number of prompts per client.
But it's like for us, there's probably a lot of value in finding a data provider.
Just the time savings.
You know, it's like that's the judgment call.
Making.
Yep.
Makes sense.
Makes sense.
And then just from a size perspective, like, I don't know if you're public with, like, your client base or how many folks you work with, like, what's kind of broad direction of size of growthx?
Sorry.
You mean number of customers?
Yeah, number of customers.
Oh, like 50?
Yeah.
Yeah, pricing and all that's public, and our revenue's public, because Marcel likes to build them public.
Oh, okay, cool.
I didn't make it that far in my research, but take a peek.
Just check his LinkedIn.
Okay.
It will be revealed.
Yeah, it will be revealed.
There was a phase where that was very popular to do, but it's cool that people that stick with it.
Okay, let me just go through a quick demo.
I'm just going to show some product.
Obviously, we won't demo the API.
I'm just Let's see here.
And, sorry, one question.
Like, how do you think about...

This like API product where you're offering kind of your data like this, like is this something you're experimenting with or something you're confident, you know, is like how you actually want to build out this company?
Yeah, we're confident in the offering that we are currently providing to a number of partners, like how much further that API expands, I think will be in question.
But like the monitoring piece of like, let's get data to people that are, I mean, maybe there's a world where we make it directly publicly available and competitors could build directly on top of it.
Like, actually, I think given the direction of the space, that's what like a lot of the, I mean, SEMrush does that in its own way right now.
And so that could make sense.
But again, I think the more interesting business is what you do on top of the monitoring platform.
So, yes, so then, like, how do you see, like, the value of, like, giving this stream away?
Because it's like, I mean, it's.
What value, right?
Like that infrastructure for you to keep up.

Is it, I mean, just like how many dollars I'm paying for this API or like, do you want it to be kind of some play where like, you know, and we're open to it, like for our customers to like get into your product and buy more.
Yeah, that, I mean, our other partner agreements have that baked in.
So we have kind of like a mutual referral situation where it's like, if we send a customer to you guys, X cut of it.
If you send a customer to us, X cut of it.
And I think that becomes more interesting as we add more product capabilities on top.
I mean, I kind of have a thesis that like, I don't know the timing of it playing out, but I think the web is going to look entirely different.
Like if 95% of content is for AI crawlers and agents taking outcomes, like everything's going to look different.
And so I think as we build more capabilities, that direction, the referral opportunity, vice versa, gets more powerful.
So that, that is a core.
And then I think we've built this out for ourselves.
It makes sense to monetize it with partners that are a good fit to scale and have a need.
And it's kind of our core competency, like our background is enterprise data.
My co-founder is like a deep data person, already came from Quantcast.
And our prior company, we only sold to the top 500 banks and insurance companies.
was huge enterprise API integrations, just like a ton of controls.
And like, we're very competent in that category.
So it's like, okay, I think a lot of people are doing this in a pretty elementary or isolated way.
We have confidence in our ability to scale it and like have it be a core part of our offering.
So a part of the question too is like, are you experimenting with this and you're going to shut it down in a year?
Like that, we haven't been talking about it as an experiment.
We've been talking about it as like, this is a core capability we need to do business.
And we have significant partners that are relying on it.
I know the space is super new and I mean, who knows, right?
Like same with us.
I think like where we want to be.
It's like, like we're kind of tool agnostic.
Yep.
Like my view or our view is like, for you to be able to do all this stuff, you kind of need an incredible team that not every company has or even wants to build.
And that's why they come to us.
So we just wanted to do best.
partnerships you have today, the ones that it's just like a data feed, are you, do you have a, are you charging on the customers or the volume or what are.
Yeah, we've been charging on customer, like customers and volume.
Like I will say, like, say again, like both dimensions.
I mean, essentially it breaks down to the volume of things you're monitoring, but we have been charging on both dimensions.
Like, okay, you're bringing in 20 entities times monitoring 300 or 500 prompts on the number of models that you want to cover.
And we've been doing kind of custom pricing based on that.
Okay.
Yeah.
And I'd say that significantly ranges.
Like some companies come in and they're like, we need a super high volume.
Yeah.

Of prompts, and we need it across all models, and then others come in and say, look, like, the reality is, like, the answers to these prompts are not changing significantly, and so, like, we don't need to be capturing at a crazy frequency, we only want one or two models, and so, right now, I think we don't have, like, a formulaic pricing approach for this, like, this has been, like, a back and forth based on company need to find something that works as we continue to do it.
Okay, but if someone works directly with you, it's basically on, it's just volume, there's no, like, flat fees or something, or platform fees, it's just, like, the volume of prompts times.
Like, if a company just signs up for the product, you mean?
Yeah.
Yeah.
It's a general pricing model.

Yeah, I mean, then, typically, there's just, like, one brand, right, like, Lenovo, Skims, Lenovo, like, they sign up as Skims to monitor X thousand prompts, and they've got their users and their personas and their competitors, and they're paying an annual.
platform fee to use it.
Okay.
Okay.
Is your pricing public on the website?
It's not.
Okay.
Not yet.
Not yet.
Probably will be in the coming weeks.
Okay.
Let's just quickly flip through this.
I got a hard stop at nine.
So I'm just, I'm in a, it's a live environment to demo work.
So Disney Plus is the pretend, pretend you're healthy Disney Plus drive growth.
Just to give you a quick sense of what we do, we simply take the brand name.
We start generating a bunch of information automatically.
So a description of what they do, personas are built out automatically of the types of people Disney Plus sells to.
So you see like the tired parent versus nostalgic pop culture fan.
These are, these folks have different prompts, different answers coming back.
So part of what we build out with our customers is who are they selling to?
What type of things are they asking?
What are the answers coming back?
Competitive brands gets auto mapped key topics and themes.
Think of these as like indicative of traditional search that helps translate into our prompt generation platform, things that you actually want.
To be monitoring.
And then this.
We're generating prompts at scale.
Companies can come in bulk upload prompts.
They can create prompts one-off.
Our prompt generation platform starts to seed it.

And there's quite a few capabilities here, filter by branded versus non-branded prompts, filter by your personas, filter by the key topics, filter by each platform, filter by the stage of the customer journey, right?
So it's like if I'm in the...
Branded in here as well.
You cut out for a little bit, Chris.
Chris?
He's gone.
He's gone.
How would they know the stage of the customer journey?
It's just based on an intent analysis of the prompt.
So like depending on what the prompt is, the same way SEMrush would do search intent for keywords, right?
Okay, it's not like linked to a CRM or something and mapping.
I don't know.
I know.
I
I'm coming back.
Can you guys hear me now?
Yeah, you're back.
I don't know what's better, me thinking the app crashed or losing Wi-Fi.
We lost Wi-Fi that time.
Quick question.
For using the platform, is it always kind of like, I have data from when I start tracking, or do you pool that a little bit?
I mean, I know you don't have that many customers, so maybe this is a future thing.
But if I pick a prompt, know for the last year what is performance.
Yeah, we're not doing any pooling right now across customers, so it's literally from the day you start.
So there's no sharing across customer environments, which is good and bad.
I think it's good because it's unique to them, but it also is we do not have historical data for an entity that comes in.
We've talked about creating some industry slices of like, you're in the CMS space, here's the things we monitor.
Yeah, mean, it seems like there's a pool around just tools, like best tool for X or Y.
Kind of would be interesting to keep, but yeah.
But you get the sense here, right?
So I'm flipping through stages of the customer journey.
If I flip to comparison, obviously Disney's in that prompt, so they're going to show up 100% of the time.
If I flip to all branded and non-branded and move to the awareness stage, they're going to show up a little bit less frequently.
This is like I'm looking for something in the subscription category, but I don't know what it is.
And so, you know, Netflix is a little more highly.
Okay.
I mean, I know you have a hard step, so maybe we can talk about next steps a little bit.
I think, like, I like how clean your product is.
Profound seems a little bit more, like, opinionated.
Yeah.
And I don't know, there's just something, like, sleek and, like, you know, weird about it.
But I like Scrunch.
Like, what could we get access to to keep exploring this?
Do you have the pricing that you could share with us?
Kirkland's the one on our side that's kind of, like, building all this.
like, if you could get access to something.
I think to kind of dig a little bit deeper, that would be great.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Why don't we do like a week access or something, Kirkland, for you so you can kind of play around, set up one environment.
We'll do a kickoff with Will on our customer success data team.
But I ultimately think like you may even want to think a little bit about like what endpoints you want from an API perspective.
So, you know, we touched on, let me just click on like one area here to give you a little bit more depth of like what you'd be pulling back from us.
Right.
So if you're looking at a prompt, you you can filter between every one of the models.
You can see what websites powered the answer.
You can see presence, position, sentiment, and you can create prompts on the fly.
Right.
So if you want to ask a new question, do it, tie it to a persona, tag it.
And then the last section from an API perspective, pretty critical is this sources capability, which is mapping all of the website's own competitive and third party.
that are in AI answers with, is your brand present?
What competitors are present?
What prompts is it tied to?
So when you think about distribution and like citations as maybe the new backlink, it's like, okay, is the brand present here?
Yes or no?
Is it our site?
Is it a competitive site?
Is it a third party site?
What's the sentiment?
So we think position, presence, sentiment, citations, those are the things that I think you'd be taking into a Looker Studio environment and then visualizing for dashboards at scale for customers.
So why don't we set you up next week to play with it for a week, just like in the interface, but then move into more discussions on consuming an API?
Because that's kind of the end game if we move forward.
That sounds great.
And from my end, I think just on the partnership level, I know you guys are working with Daydream.
Like it's critical for me that whoever we use here, like this partnership, I want it to go deeper.
Like when people think of AISCO, I want them to stop.
I don't even know who they think about today, but I want that to be us and whoever that partner is.
I know you work with Daydream, so maybe this is something we can speak about a little bit more, but I want webinars, reports, just deep thought in the space.
I want it to be Graphite, Daydream, whatever.
I want it to be us and whoever we partner with.
So yeah, I would love to talk more about that because the data, to your point, is commoditized, right?
I think this brand is important.
Super open to doing joint webinars, joint webpapers.
I would definitely not insist on that being branded across our client base.
Unique efforts that we take with you, we would do separately.
Obviously, we're not going to be exclusive in who is consuming our API.
If they want to take insights and publish it, great, they can go do that.
But distribution is critical for us.
If we have an opportunity to speak publicly about insights or do webinars together, certainly open to it.
Okay.
And how big are you guys at this point?
How do we define big people?
Yeah, I guess people.
And then you guys are seed stage.
I think I already saw that.
Yeah, I guess at this point, it's just people.
And if you can tell, like a rough size of like, I mean, you said 60 customers, so I can probably just guess, but maybe just people.
We're not sharing any revenue numbers yet.
We've been super lean.
So we did a $4 million seed run with Mayfield.
We will be like 11 people at the end of April here.
We were three people two months ago.
So like we stayed really lean through the first year of building.
And one of the benefits of building again is like head of sales, worked with for five years, head of growth and marketing, worked with five years.
My co-founder, we did 12 years at last company.
We spent 12 years together, sold it to Yaks, 300 people, 60 in revenue.
Like we know how to build platforms and teams for the long haul.
And so I think measuring people is a weird thing in today's environment, given how efficient you can build technology and scale things.
But that gives you a little sense of kind of where we are.
Yeah, that makes sense.
And then on your team, are you the person that's thinking like the deepest about like brand and marketing and kind of like how your go-to-market works out?
Yes and no.
I mean, my attention is definitely split.
I've hired a head of marketing and growth, this woman named Colleen, that would certainly be involved in kind of like joint press and marketing and thought leadership webinars.
So like she would, she would help spearhead that effort.
I'd probably be speaking along with the representative from the partner, but I have a woman running marketing and growth on the team.
Sounds good.
Great to check us and Kirkland will connect next week with Will.
Yeah, that sounds great.
Thanks guys.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential partnership between GrowthX and Scrunch AI for AI-powered SEO monitoring and insights.](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Scrunch AI offers comprehensive AI search monitoring, including presence, position, sentiment, and citations across multiple AI platforms](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=379.0)
  - [GrowthX seeks a data provider to enhance their SEO offerings for clients, with interest in deeper partnership opportunities](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=73.0)
  - [Scrunch AI views monitoring as a commodity and is building additional capabilities on top of their core monitoring platform](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=363.0)
  - [Both parties expressed interest in exploring an API-based partnership with potential for joint marketing efforts](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1721.0)

## Topics

### Scrunch AI's Offering and Market Position

  - [Started 18 months ago, focusing on AI-powered web search monitoring](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=336.0)
  - [Offers enterprise SaaS deals ($50K-$100K/year) and mid-market month-to-month packages ($300-$1000/month)](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=396.0)
  - [Monitoring covers GPT, Perplexity, Meta, Claude, and soon Gemini](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=525.0)
  - [Developed a prompt generation platform considering various inputs (e.g., AdWord data, customer interviews)](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=742.0)
  - [Emphasizes persona-based approach for more accurate and relevant monitoring](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1407.0)

### GrowthX's Needs and Market Approach

  - [Serves \~50 customers, offering growth services "on steroids" with AI integration](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=103.0)
  - [Seeks comprehensive AI SEO monitoring solution for clients](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=125.0)
  - [Interested in distribution strategies to increase client mentions and citations](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=157.0)
  - [Looking for a partner to provide data and insights, potentially integrating into their own dashboard solution](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=103.0)

### Data Partnership and API Offering

  - [Scrunch AI provides API access to partners for monitoring data](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=488.0)
  - [Key data points: presence, position, sentiment, and comprehensive citations](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=507.0)
  - [Pricing based on customers and volume of monitored prompts/models](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1287.0)
  - [Open to mutual referral agreements and joint marketing efforts](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1160.0)

### Product Demo Highlights

  - [Automatic generation of brand information, personas, and competitive landscape](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1405.0)
  - [Flexible prompt creation and filtering options (e.g., by persona, topic, platform, customer journey stage)](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1440.0)
  - [Detailed analysis of presence, position, sentiment, and citations for each prompt](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1659.0)

### Future Directions and Differentiation

  - [Scrunch AI building additional capabilities beyond monitoring (e.g., infrastructure for AI crawlers, content creation insights)](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=597.0)
  - [Exploring ways to provide more value through insights and action recommendations](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1176.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Scrunch AI to provide GrowthX (Kirkland) with 1-week access to the platform for evaluation](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1627.0)
  - [Schedule a kickoff meeting with Scrunch AI's customer success team (Will) to set up the trial environment](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1635.0)
  - [Discuss potential API endpoints and data consumption for GrowthX's needs](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1639.0)
  - [Explore deeper partnership opportunities, including joint webinars, reports, and thought leadership content](https://fathom.video/share/fFyzYtxhzeTscibg9Qc5PngxYz1HDsex?tab=summary&timestamp=1721.0)

action_items.description: Set up 1-week trial access to Scrunch platform; schedule kickoff call w/ Will (Scrunch CS)
Arrange 1-week Scrunch trial access for Kirkland; have Will schedule kickoff call
Follow up w/ Chris re: deeper partnership opps (webinars, reports, thought leadership)
